mockInterviews.title: Meta SQL: Window Functions & Cohort Analysis

questions.question: Write a query to calculate 7-day rolling retention by signup cohort (users who came back within 7 days of first visit)
questions.expectedAnswer: First identify each user's first event date using MIN(event_date) grouped by user_id. Then join back to events to find users who have any event within 7 days of their cohort date. Calculate retention rate as retained users / cohort size per cohort day. Use a CTE approach: first_events CTE for cohort dates, then LEFT JOIN to events with a date range condition (BETWEEN cohort_date AND cohort_date + INTERVAL '7' DAY), then aggregate by cohort_date with COUNT(DISTINCT retained users) / COUNT(DISTINCT cohort users).
followUpQuestions: How would this perform on 10 billion rows?
How would you handle late-arriving data?
What index or partition strategy would you use?

questions.question: Given a events table with user_id, event_time, event_type — write a sessionization query that groups events within 30-minute windows
questions.expectedAnswer: Use LAG() to compute the time gap between consecutive events per user. Flag a new session start when the gap exceeds 30 minutes or it is the first event for that user. Use SUM() of the session_start flags as a window function to assign a session number per user. Then aggregate by user_id and session_id to get session_start (MIN of event timestamps), session_end (MAX), and event_count (COUNT). The session_id can be a composite of user_id + session number.
followUpQuestions: How does this query perform on a partitioned Hive table with 10 billion rows?
How would you handle late-arriving events that belong to a past session?
What if the inactivity threshold varies by user segment?

questions.question: Find the top 3 users by total spend per country, breaking ties by most recent transaction
questions.expectedAnswer: Use a CTE to aggregate total spend per user per country with SUM(amount). Then apply ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY country ORDER BY total_spend DESC, MAX(created_at) DESC) to rank users within each country. Filter WHERE rank <= 3. ROW_NUMBER is preferred over RANK here to get exactly 3 per country. The tie-breaking by most recent transaction ensures deterministic ordering.
followUpQuestions: What if you wanted DENSE_RANK instead — how does the output differ?
How would you optimize this on a table with 1 billion transactions?
What partition strategy would you use for the transactions table?

questions.question: Given a raw events stream with duplicate events (same user_id + event_id), deduplicate keeping the latest record per event_id
questions.expectedAnswer: Use ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY event_id ORDER BY created_at DESC) to rank events within each event_id group. Wrap in a CTE and select WHERE row_num = 1 to keep the latest record. For Presto/Hive at scale, the ROW_NUMBER approach is cleaner and allows the optimizer to prune partitions. The QUALIFY clause (supported in Presto, BigQuery, Snowflake) can eliminate the CTE for a more concise query. In production, prefer idempotent pipeline design so deduplication happens at write time.
followUpQuestions: At petabyte scale, what's the cost of ROW_NUMBER on an unpartitioned table?
How would you make the pipeline idempotent so this deduplication is never needed?
What if two events have identical event_id and created_at — how do you break ties?

mockInterviews.title: Meta Data Modeling: Instagram Feed Analytics

questions.question: Design a fact table for Instagram post impressions at Meta scale — what's the grain? What dimensions?
questions.expectedAnswer: The grain is one row per impression. Fact table: fact_post_impressions(impression_id, post_id, user_id, device_id, impression_timestamp, content_type, source). Dimension tables: dim_posts(post_id, creator_id, post_type, created_at), dim_users(user_id, signup_date, country, age_bucket), dim_devices(device_id, device_type, os, app_version). Partition fact table by impression_date for query performance. At Meta scale (billions of impressions/day), pre-aggregate to daily summaries for common queries.
followUpQuestions: What if a post can belong to multiple campaigns?
How do you handle deletes in an immutable event stream?
What's the tradeoff between daily partitioning vs monthly?

questions.question: How would you handle creator profile changes over time (SCD Type 2)?
questions.expectedAnswer: In dim_creators, instead of updating the existing row, insert a new row with the updated profile data and set valid_from to today. Set the previous row's valid_to to today - 1 day. The impression fact table rows remain unchanged — they still point to the creator_id which now has a date range covering the historical period. Queries needing the profile at time of impression join dim_creators on creator_id AND impression_date BETWEEN valid_from AND valid_to. This preserves historical accuracy without touching the fact table. In dbt, use snapshot strategy with check_cols or timestamp strategy.
followUpQuestions: When would you choose SCD Type 1 over Type 2?
How does dbt handle SCD Type 2 — what snapshot strategy would you use?
What is the performance impact of the date-range join on a billion-row fact table?

questions.question: The product team wants to analyze 'reach vs engagement' by content type, country, and time — design the model
questions.expectedAnswer: Build two aggregate tables: agg_daily_reach (grain: content_type + country + date, metrics: unique_viewers, total_impressions) and agg_daily_engagement (grain: content_type + country + date, metrics: likes, comments, shares, saves, avg_time_spent). Join these on the shared dimensions for the analysis. For reach, use COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) which requires HyperLogLog approximation at Meta scale. For engagement rate, calculate as (likes + comments + shares) / unique_viewers. Pre-aggregate daily to avoid expensive DISTINCT operations at query time.
followUpQuestions: How accurate is HyperLogLog for reach estimation?
How would you handle users who span multiple countries?
What if the product team wants real-time reach numbers?

questions.question: How would you make this model incremental? What's the idempotency key?
questions.expectedAnswer: The idempotency key for the fact table is impression_id — each impression is unique and immutable. For incremental loads, use the impression_timestamp to select only new records since the last load: WHERE impression_timestamp > (SELECT MAX(impression_timestamp) FROM target). In dbt, configure the model with materialized='incremental', unique_key='impression_id', and an is_incremental() block. For the aggregate tables, use INSERT OVERWRITE on the date partition — this makes the pipeline idempotent because re-running for the same date produces identical results. The MERGE pattern (dbt's default for Snowflake incremental) handles late arrivals gracefully.
followUpQuestions: What happens if the source has late-arriving events?
How do you handle backfills without duplicating data?
What's the difference between append-only and merge incremental strategies?

mockInterviews.title: Meta Pipeline Design: Ads Impression Pipeline

questions.question: Design a pipeline that processes 1 trillion ad impressions per day — walk me through the architecture
questions.expectedAnswer: Events flow from ad serving into Scribe (Meta's logging infrastructure). A Flink streaming job reads from Scribe, applies deduplication using impression_id, validates the schema, enriches with dimension data (ad metadata, user segments), and writes micro-batches to Hive tables partitioned by impression_date and hour. A separate hourly batch pipeline computes billing aggregates for advertisers. A daily reconciliation job handles late-arriving clicks (up to 24h after impression). For exactly-once semantics: use idempotent writes with impression_id as the dedup key, and commit Flink checkpoints only after successful writes.
followUpQuestions: What if the upstream Scribe logs are delayed?
How do you handle schema evolution in the pipeline?
Walk me through your monitoring/alerting strategy

questions.question: How do you guarantee exactly-once delivery in a distributed pipeline?
questions.expectedAnswer: True exactly-once is impossible in a distributed system (per FLP impossibility), but effectively-once can be achieved through idempotent processing. Three strategies: (1) Idempotent writes — use a deterministic key (impression_id) and UPSERT/MERGE semantics so re-processing the same event is a no-op. (2) Transactional commits — Flink's two-phase commit protocol ensures the offset advance and data write happen atomically. (3) Deduplication at read time — if at-least-once delivery is unavoidable, deduplicate downstream using ROW_NUMBER() PARTITION BY event_id. At Meta's scale, strategy 1 (idempotent writes) is preferred because it's simpler to reason about and doesn't require distributed transactions.
followUpQuestions: What's the performance cost of UPSERT vs INSERT at 1 trillion rows?
How does Flink's checkpointing mechanism work?
What happens if the dedup key has clock skew?

questions.question: The pipeline is 4 hours late — what's your incident response?
questions.expectedAnswer: Step 1: Assess impact — which downstream consumers are affected (billing? dashboards? ML features?). Step 2: Identify the bottleneck — check Flink job metrics (backpressure, checkpoint failures, task manager restarts), Scribe lag, and sink write latency. Step 3: Communicate — notify stakeholders with ETA and impact scope. Step 4: Fix — if it's a resource issue, scale up Flink parallelism. If it's a data issue (poison record), skip the bad partition and backfill later. If it's an infrastructure issue, failover to the secondary cluster. Step 5: Post-mortem — document root cause, add monitoring for earlier detection, and update runbooks.
followUpQuestions: How do you prevent this from happening again?
What monitoring would have caught this earlier?
How do you handle the backfill of the 4 missing hours?

questions.question: How would you partition the impressions data for optimal Presto query performance?
questions.expectedAnswer: Primary partition: impression_date (DATE) — most queries filter by date range, and this allows partition pruning to scan only relevant days. Secondary partition: hour (INT, 0-23) — for intra-day queries and SLA monitoring. Within each partition, sort by advertiser_id for billing queries or user_id for targeting queries (choose based on dominant query pattern). Use ORC or Parquet columnar format with compression (Zstandard). For a 1 trillion row/day table, each hourly partition contains ~42 billion rows — consider sub-partitioning by country or advertiser_id bucket if queries consistently filter on those dimensions.
followUpQuestions: What's the tradeoff of too many partitions?
How do you handle small files from micro-batch writes?
When would you use bucketing vs partitioning?

mockInterviews.title: Meta Product Sense: Measuring Feature Success

questions.question: Meta launches a new feature that lets users react to Stories with audio. How do you measure success?
questions.expectedAnswer: Define success from multiple angles. Adoption: % of DAU who send at least one audio reaction in first 30 days. Usage: average audio reactions sent per user per week, average duration of audio reactions. Quality: % of audio reactions that are listened to by the recipient (completion rate). Retention impact: 30-day retention of audio reaction users vs. non-users (controlling for selection bias with propensity matching). Guardrail metrics: overall Stories engagement (should not cannibalize text/emoji reactions), report rate (audio could be used for harassment). North star: if audio reactions increase meaningful interactions between close friends, 'meaningful social interactions' metric should improve. Run A/B test with holdout group for 4-6 weeks.
followUpQuestions: How do you separate novelty effect from real signal?
What's your minimum detectable effect for this experiment?
How long would you run the test?

questions.question: You run an A/B test on Feed ranking — control shows +5% CTR but -3% session length. What do you recommend?
questions.expectedAnswer: This is a classic engagement quality tradeoff. Higher CTR with lower session length suggests the new ranking is showing more clickbait — users click more but leave sooner. Recommendation: do NOT ship based on CTR alone. Analyze deeper: (1) What content types are getting more clicks? If it's low-quality content, the ranking change is harmful. (2) Check long-term retention (7-day, 30-day) — session length is a better proxy for satisfaction than CTR. (3) Look at composition of clicks — are they meaningful interactions (comments, shares) or passive clicks? (4) Check downstream metrics: ad revenue per session (shorter sessions = less ad inventory). Decision framework: if CTR increase comes from quality content and retention is flat or up, consider shipping. If retention drops, reject the change.
followUpQuestions: What if the PM insists on shipping because CTR is the team's OKR?
How do you define 'quality' content in an objective, measurable way?
What other metrics would you check before making a decision?

questions.question: Define a North Star metric for Instagram Reels. What guardrail metrics would you track?
questions.expectedAnswer: North Star: Daily Active Reel Viewers (DARV) — users who watch at least one Reel per day. This captures both content supply (creators making Reels) and demand (users watching). Guardrail metrics: (1) Creator-side: daily active Reel creators, average Reels published per creator per week — ensures content supply isn't declining. (2) Quality: average completion rate (% of Reel watched), average watch time per Reel — ensures views are meaningful, not scroll-past. (3) Ecosystem health: ratio of Reels time to Feed/Stories time — ensure Reels isn't cannibalizing other surfaces in a way that harms overall engagement. (4) Revenue: ad impressions per Reel session, CPM — ensure monetization isn't degrading. (5) Safety: % of Reels flagged/removed for policy violations.
followUpQuestions: Why DARV over total Reel views?
What if DARV goes up but completion rate drops?
How do you balance creator growth vs viewer growth?

questions.question: The daily active users metric drops 10% on Sunday. Is this a problem?
questions.expectedAnswer: Not necessarily — this requires context. Step 1: Check historical pattern — is a Sunday dip normal? Compare to the past 4 Sundays and the same Sunday last year. If DAU consistently drops 8-12% on Sundays, a 10% drop is within normal range. Step 2: Check day-of-week seasonality across all metrics (sessions, time spent, etc.) — if all metrics show a consistent Sunday pattern, it's user behavior, not a product issue. Step 3: Segment analysis — does the dip affect all user segments equally? If it's concentrated in one country or demographic, investigate local factors (holidays, competitor events). Step 4: Only if the dip is anomalous compared to historical Sunday patterns should you treat it as an incident. The real signal is week-over-week same-day comparison, not day-over-day.
followUpQuestions: What if the dip is 25% instead of 10%?
How would you build automated anomaly detection for DAU?
What other metrics would you check to confirm it's not a real problem?

questions.question: Tell me about a time you identified a problem before anyone asked you to and drove the solution to completion
questions.expectedAnswer: Strong E5 answers demonstrate: identifying the problem independently (not just executing), building a case with data (not opinion), influencing peers and leadership, and measuring the outcome. Example structure: at Simplex, SSIS was used for all ETL but had no version control, no testing, and 6-hour deployment cycles. I noticed this was causing recurring production issues and proposed migrating to dbt. I built a POC with 3 high-priority models, showed it ran 40% faster, had full Git history, and could be deployed in minutes. Got buy-in from the engineering lead. Trained the team over 3 sprints. Result: 248 models in production, 97% incremental, deployment from 6 hours to 15 minutes.
followUpQuestions: What would you do differently?
How did you measure the impact?
What would you have done if you hadn't gotten buy-in?

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to convince skeptical engineers to adopt a new tool or approach
questions.expectedAnswer: E5 ownership means influencing without authority. A strong answer shows: understanding the resistance (people fear change and unknown risk), addressing it with evidence (POC, metrics, gradual rollout), and following through post-adoption. Example: at Nuvei, I introduced dbt to a team that had been using SSIS for years. The resistance was real — SSIS was familiar, dbt was unknown, and the team worried about learning curve. I built a custom dbt materialization for SingleStore (their non-standard DB), which showed I was invested in making it work for their specific setup. Then I ran a hands-on workshop, pair-programmed the first 5 models with team members, and set up CI/CD so they could see tests passing. Within 2 months, the entire team was writing dbt models independently.
followUpQuestions: What if the team still refused after your POC?
How did you handle the person who was most resistant?
What metrics did you use to show the adoption was successful?

questions.question: Tell me about the most technically complex data system you've built. What were the hardest tradeoffs?
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong E5 answer goes deep on technical decisions and explicitly names the tradeoffs. Example: the observability dashboard serving 784K Azure DevOps work items at 200ms response time. The hard tradeoffs: (1) Cost vs latency — commercial tools cost $150K/yr, so I built a custom solution with 3-tier caching: frozen blobs for historical data, monthly partitions for recent data, live queries for today only. (2) Freshness vs performance — business wanted real-time but querying 784K items live was too slow. Solution: materialized views refreshed every 15 minutes for dashboards, with live queries available for drill-down. (3) Complexity vs maintainability — the 3-tier architecture is more complex than a single query, but each tier is independently testable and the system has been running in production with zero maintenance for 6 months.
followUpQuestions: What would break first if scale doubled?
How would you hand this system off to another engineer?
What monitoring did you put in place?

questions.question: Give me an example of when you unblocked an entire team by solving a technical problem
questions.expectedAnswer: E5 impact is measured by unblocking others, not just your own output. Example: during the Snowflake-to-Databricks acquisition validation, the acquiring company's team was stuck — they had 12.6M records to reconcile but no framework for cross-platform validation. I built a validation pipeline with defensive schema mapping (unmapped columns logged and excluded rather than failing), automated row count comparison, and statistical sampling for data accuracy checks. The key decision: I chose to validate in batches of 100K records rather than all at once, so the business team could start reviewing partial results while the full validation ran. This unblocked the M&A due diligence process by 2 weeks. The result: 99.8% accuracy confirmed, $0.005/day compute cost.
followUpQuestions: How did you prioritize which tables to validate first?
What would you have done if accuracy was only 95%?
How did you communicate progress to non-technical stakeholders?
